evidence_quotes.quote: With video games, this can be fairly easy to do
I've experienced this several times with games I've purchased a few years ago.

As of now, companies can launch a game, kill it's online services after a week of it's launch, and users who spent money on it will be completely unprotected with no guarantee of a refund or way to get it back up through their own means
Today it's video games having this problem, tomorrow it'll be any other digital products, computers, cars, appliances etc. that require a connection to any official company server.

paragraphs.text: I'd like to use this medium to suggest looking into ownership of digital products, including but not exclusively video games. We've unfortunately come to a time frame where a product which has online functionality and capabilities ends it's lifecycle. The issue is that, the money paid to own these products was still spent and will never be given back. Understandably, companies can't keep online services for their products forever, especially if they're no longer profitable, however they should provide or allow users that might still use that prouct to have the ability to host or find a workaround themselves to ensure it's online features are still up. With video games, this can be fairly easy to do, however companies will find themselves able to shut down any attempts to revive any sort of online functionality for the game even if they have essentially abandoned it and don't want to provide a way or to use the online capabilities of this game. I've experienced this several times with games I've purchased a few years ago. Please note that I no longer buy games on a regular basis, and as such play these same old games I've purchased years ago, as such, more than 90% of my game library is old games. Many of them no longer have working official online servers from the companies that published them, however some have communities who took it to themselves to bring the game back up with some workarounds. The issue with this, is that there's no law that guarantees or protects people who like to revive and regain access to products they've purchased, there are no laws that can determine how long a company should keep a product online for or offer any alternative solutions once the official servers are brought offline. As of now, companies can launch a game, kill it's online services after a week of it's launch, and users who spent money on it will be completely unprotected with no guarantee of a refund or way to get it back up through their own means, many times getting into problems with the company's legal department if any attempts to bring it back online using their own private servers are made. I strongly urge you to please look into this matter. Today it's video games having this problem, tomorrow it'll be any other digital products, computers, cars, appliances etc. that require a connection to any official company server. Many thanks to anyone involved in this initiative for providing this opportunity. I and I'm sure all of us (EU member citizens or not) really appreciate having a chance to be heard.
